label: SES-110 / CNV-012 close-out — concurrent-write-safety architecture, draft phase. Authors DEC-325 (SessionDraftToken: per-session capability token for draft-phase write authorization with rotation-on-handoff and reaper-on-expiry) and files PI-100 (the deferred follow-on conversation: concurrency on promoted records + storage-substrate validation at scale), with kickoff WT-056. No code shipped; design + governance records + next-conversation kickoff. Attaches to WS-012.

session.session_title: Concurrent-write-safety architecture (draft phase) — SessionDraftToken decided; promoted-record concurrency + substrate deferred
session.session_description:
Single Claude.ai web (sandbox) conversation. CNV-012. Parent workstream WS-012 (Parallel agent orchestrator).

Topics covered:

Seed prompt: "We need to address the ID collision problems being encountered in multiple do sessions lately."

1. Diagnosed the recurring identifier collisions against the live codebase, not memory. Root cause: governance close-out payloads bake EXPLICIT identifiers (DEC-NNN, PI-NNN) chosen read-time against a possibly-stale snapshot, while the access layer already supports collision-safe server-assignment (next_prefixed_identifier + SAVEPOINT-retry). Confirmed the migration is half-done: sessions adopted server-assignment (~ses_099) but decisions/PIs still bake explicit IDs because the references section needs stable handles. Evidence of live collisions in the repo: ses_098_dogfood_phase2.PENDING_REKEY.json and ses_100.json both claim DEC-319..DEC-324; git history shows three recent re-key episodes (SES-095/096, 097/098, 098/100).

2. Surfaced a companion correctness bug: apply_close_out.py treats HTTP 409 as "already present - skipping" and exits 0, so a genuine collision (same identifier, different content) is silently dropped rather than failing loudly.

3. Doug set the governing design constraint: design the concurrency-safety solution WITHOUT regard to which tool can interact. Live participation is the admission rule - a client that cannot follow the rules does not get to write; we either fix the MCP path or use other tools.

4. Confirmed transport reality: claude.ai-web via remote MCP connector is blocked upstream (DEC-244 / PI-045 / PI-049, the ofid_ OAuth token-exchange bug). A live tool-load attempt from this conversation returned only github tools, confirming the CRM Builder MCP is not reachable here. Live-capable surfaces today: Claude Desktop (stdio MCP) and the chat-UI client (PI-052, in build); browser claude.ai only once Anthropic ships the connector fix.

5. Decomposed the architecture into three layers: (1) crash-safe correctness floor - server-assigned identifiers for creates plus a write-precondition for modifies - which never leaks state; (2) advisory coordination registry - the check-out / who-holds-what; (3) admission gate - governance writes require an open live-session context.

6. Reframed drafts as the STAGING mechanism for long-lived, multi-record agent work on a short-transaction substrate: an agent accumulates a unit of work as short writes in draft, then promotes atomically or discards on abandonment. Established that draft records should be private to their owner - invisible and non-referenceable to peer agents, writable only by the owner plus a privileged lifecycle (reaper) role. The only legitimate non-owner writer is the reaper reclaiming an abandoned draft.

7. DECISION (DEC-325): draft-phase write authorization via a per-session capability token, the SessionDraftToken. One token per session, minted at session open, stamped on each draft via a direct FK (draft_token_id) set atomically at create; every draft write must present the token; handoff is server-side token rotation (new secret) so the prior holder is locked out; the token expires to a reaper that reclaims abandoned drafts without it. Session-scope (not per-record), direct FK (not a references edge, per DEC-199), token excluded from all logged output (DEC-164 logs are git-tracked).

8. Confirmed substrate facts in db.py: SQLite with serialized writers (BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout=5000, isolation_level=None), no WAL, one DB file per engagement. Concurrency is therefore per-engagement: across engagements it scales horizontally for free; within one engagement everything funnels through a single serialized writer. Distinguished logical concurrency (many partitioned agents, short writes - SQLite survives) from throughput concurrency (sustained rate beyond one writer - SQLite does not).

9. Deferred two open threads to a separate conversation (PI-100): concurrency control for PROMOTED (already-real) records once the token retires, and substrate validation at the target peak number of concurrent writers against a single engagement.

In flight at end:
- The two deferred threads are captured in PI-100 with kickoff WT-056; nothing left dangling.
- Complementary existing planning items under WS-012, noted but NOT duplicated: PI-077 (claimed_by / claimed_at on planning_items), PI-078 (identifier reservation API endpoint), PI-079 (orchestration ready-batches). DEC-325's SessionDraftToken is a distinct, complementary facet (draft-phase write authorization), not a replacement for any of these.
- The 409-as-silent-skip correctness bug in apply_close_out.py was surfaced but not filed as its own PI this conversation; it belongs to the same concurrency-safety effort and should be picked up alongside PI-100 (noted in PI-100's description).
session.session_executive_summary: Designed the draft phase of a concurrent-write-safety model for the V2 governance database, prompted by recurring identifier collisions traced to explicit read-time identifier allocation in close-out payloads and an apply path that silently skips genuine collisions. Decided on a SessionDraftToken: one per-session capability token, stamped on each draft record, required to edit it, rotated on handoff so ownership transfers cleanly, and expiring to a cleanup process for abandoned work. Correctness rests on a crash-safe floor (server-assigned identifiers plus draft staging) so a lost token degrades coordination, not data integrity. Concurrency on promoted records and storage-substrate validation at target scale were deferred to a follow-on conversation (PI-100).

conversation.conversation_title: Concurrency-safety design — from identifier collisions to the SessionDraftToken draft model
conversation.conversation_purpose: Diagnose the recurring governance identifier collisions and design a concurrency-safety model that supports a high number of AI agents writing concurrently while guaranteeing data safety. Settle the draft-phase ownership/authorization mechanism.
conversation.conversation_description: Single-conversation Claude.ai web chat. Began from the seed prompt about ID collisions in multiple sessions; diagnosed the root cause (explicit read-time identifier baking + 409-as-benign-skip) against the live codebase; established the governing constraint (design without regard to tool access; live participation is the admission rule); confirmed transport reality (claude.ai-web MCP blocked upstream, Desktop stdio + chat-UI client live-capable); decomposed into a three-layer model (crash-safe floor / advisory coordination / admission gate); reframed drafts as the staging mechanism for long-lived multi-record agent work; and decided DEC-325 (SessionDraftToken). Two threads — concurrency on promoted records and substrate validation at scale — were explicitly deferred to a separate conversation and filed as PI-100.

work_tickets.work_ticket_title: Kickoff — concurrency for promoted records + substrate validation at scale (opens against PI-100)
work_tickets.work_ticket_description: Kickoff prompt for the follow-on conversation deferred from CNV-012. Frames the two open threads (promoted-record modify-modify mechanism once the draft token retires; SQLite-per-engagement vs alternative store at the target peak concurrent-writer count) plus the companion apply_close_out 409-skip fix, carries forward the facts already established in CNV-012 so they are not re-derived, and sets ARCHITECTURE operating mode. Addresses PI-100.

planning_items.title: Settle concurrency control for promoted (already-real) governance records and validate the storage substrate at target write-concurrency scale
planning_items.description: The two open threads from CNV-012, deferred by Doug to a separate conversation. THREAD 1 - concurrency on PROMOTED records: once a draft is promoted to a real record the SessionDraftToken (DEC-325) retires, so real records need their own modify-modify protection that guarantees no lost updates. Choose the mechanism: optimistic version/precondition check (reuse the existing updated_at column with a compare-and-swap, or add a dedicated monotonic row_version - note no row_version stamp exists today; only charter/status/reference_book carry a different 'version' concept) versus an advisory lease over the crash-safe floor versus a hybrid. THREAD 2 - substrate at scale: the engine is SQLite with serialized writers (BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout=5000, no WAL) and one DB file per engagement, so write concurrency is per-engagement. Decide whether SQLite-per-engagement holds at the target peak number of concurrent writers against a SINGLE engagement, or whether a different store (e.g., Postgres with row-level MVCC) is required - distinguishing logical concurrency (many partitioned agents, short writes - SQLite survives) from throughput concurrency (sustained rate beyond a single writer - SQLite does not). Also fold in the companion fix surfaced in CNV-012: apply_close_out.py treats HTTP 409 as benign skip and exits 0, silently dropping genuine collisions; it should hard-fail when an identifier matches but content differs. Resolves when the full concurrent-write-safety model is settled: draft phase (DEC-325) + promoted-record phase + substrate decision. Complementary to PI-077 / PI-078 / PI-079 under WS-012; opens against WT-056.
planning_items.executive_summary: The two concurrency threads deferred from CNV-012. Thread 1, promoted records: choose the modify-modify protection for already-real records once the SessionDraftToken (DEC-325) retires, among an optimistic version/precondition check, an advisory lease, or a hybrid. Thread 2, substrate: validate whether SQLite-per-engagement (serialized writers, BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout, no WAL) holds at the target peak concurrent-writer count against one engagement, or whether a store with row-level MVCC such as Postgres is required. Also folds in the CNV-012 companion fix: apply_close_out.py treats HTTP 409 as a benign skip and exits 0, silently dropping genuine collisions, and should hard-fail when an identifier matches but content differs.

decisions.title: Draft-phase write authorization via a per-session capability token (SessionDraftToken)
decisions.context: High-concurrency multi-agent writing is a primary goal for the V2 governance system, but data safety is non-negotiable - an unsafe system is useless. Recurring identifier collisions trace to close-out payloads baking explicit identifiers chosen read-time against a stale snapshot, compounded by an apply path that treats 409 as benign. The agreed shape puts correctness on a crash-safe floor (server-assigned identifiers for creates; a write-precondition for modifies) and treats drafts as the staging area that lets a long-lived, multi-record agent unit of work commit or discard atomically on a short-transaction (SQLite, serialized-writer) substrate. Drafts must be private to their creating agent. The open question this decision settles: how is write authorization to draft records enforced, and how does an explicit ownership handoff work.
decisions.decision: Enforce draft-phase write authorization with a per-session capability token, the SessionDraftToken. (1) One token is minted server-side when a session opens and returned to the agent. (2) Every draft record the agent creates is stamped with a direct foreign key to the token row (draft_token_id), set atomically in the same INSERT - so the object's authorizing token is a single local lookup, no graph traversal. (3) Every write to a draft must present the token; the server compares the object's token to the presented one and blocks on mismatch or absence. (4) Handoff is a server-side rotation: the new owner presents the current token and requests transfer, the server issues a fresh secret and invalidates the old one - a true transfer, not a copy. (5) The token expires after a TTL; a privileged lifecycle/reaper role can cancel or reclaim expired/abandoned drafts WITHOUT the token. (6) Drafts are private to their owner: invisible and non-referenceable to peer agents, while the identifier allocator (not a peer) still sees draft-claimed identifiers so allocation skips them. Token scope is the SESSION (the ownership unit per DEC-306). The linkage is a FK COLUMN, not a references-table edge (DEC-199 frequency-denormalization precedent), which also avoids the create-then-edge ordering window. The token never appears in logged output (DEC-164 deposit/apply logs are git-tracked).
decisions.rationale: A capability token makes ownership explicit and checkable: the holder can write, peers cannot, by construction - no per-agent identity/ACL system needed. Session-scope matches the per-agent ownership unit (DEC-306, one chat = one session) and makes handoff atomic - one token covers all of a session's drafts, so the whole unit of work changes hands at once. A direct FK to the token row keeps the hot-path write check a single local comparison and makes rotation O(1) (update one secret; every draft's FK stays valid) rather than O(N) re-stamping. Because correctness rests on the crash-safe floor (server-assigned IDs + draft staging), a lost or leaked token degrades coordination, not correctness: an abandoned draft is simply reaped. This is what lets the model tolerate the non-deterministic, frequently-abandoned lifecycle of agent conversations without the orphaned-lock wedge that pure pessimistic locking would guarantee.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Per-record token - rejected: an agent juggles N secrets and handoff must rewrite N objects. (b) Derive authorization by traversing object -> session -> token - rejected: multi-hop fragility (a draft decision links to a conversation, two hops; the intermediate may itself be draft) plus the create-then-edge ordering window the codebase already patches with _inline_membership_edges. (c) Stamp the raw token VALUE on each object - rejected: handoff becomes O(N) re-stamping versus O(1) row rotation. (d) Pure pessimistic lock (block until release) as the CORRECTNESS mechanism - rejected for the draft phase: non-deterministic agent lifecycles guarantee orphaned-lock wedges; the token governs only the draft phase while correctness rests on the crash-safe floor. (e) Reuse/extend the existing concurrency PIs (PI-077 claimed_by/claimed_at, PI-078 identifier reservation) - these are complementary facets, not substitutes; the SessionDraftToken is specifically the draft-phase write-authorization mechanism and does not duplicate them.
decisions.consequences: Scope is the DRAFT phase only. Once a draft is promoted to a real record the token retires and edits fall under the normal regime, where the modify-modify mechanism (optimistic version/precondition vs advisory lease) is still UNRESOLVED - filed as PI-100. Implementation requires a SessionDraftToken table, a draft_token_id FK column on draft-capable entity tables, a write-path token check, and rotation/expiry/reaper logic; it should be built only after the full model (promoted-record half + substrate decision) is settled, since the two phases are one system. Complementary to PI-077 / PI-078 / PI-079 under WS-012. No new relationship vocabulary is required (FK column, not an edge). The companion apply-path bug (409 treated as benign skip, masking real collisions) should be fixed alongside.
decisions.executive_summary: Draft-phase writes to the V2 governance database are authorized by a per-session capability token, the SessionDraftToken: one token minted at session open, stamped on each draft via a direct draft_token_id FK set at create, and required on every draft write. Handoff is a server-side rotation that issues a fresh secret and locks out the prior holder; the token expires to a reaper that reclaims abandoned drafts without it. Scope is the session (DEC-306); the linkage is an FK column, not a references edge (DEC-199); the token is excluded from logged output (DEC-164). Because correctness rests on the crash-safe floor of server-assigned identifiers plus draft staging, a lost token degrades coordination, not integrity. Promoted-record concurrency and substrate validation are deferred to PI-100.
